edges.target: Normal chest radiograph in Pontiac fever case

nodes.description: Aerosol-producing water systems transfer Legionella from colonized water to air, enabling inhalational exposure through aerosols capable of reaching the tracheobronchial tree.

evidence.reference_title: [Occurrence and pathogenicity of the family of Legionellaceae].
evidence.snippet: Aerosol-generating systems aid in the transmission of Legionella from water to the air. Human inhalation of contaminated aerosols leads to Legionella infection and disease outbreaks.

evidence.reference_title: An outbreak of Pontiac fever related to whirlpool use, Michigan 1982.
evidence.snippet: Aerosol size studies show that the whirlpool aerator produced water droplets small enough to travel deep into the tracheobronchial tree but large enough to transport L pneumophila.

nodes.description: Pontiac fever develops after a short incubation as an acute febrile, influenza-like illness with constitutional symptoms rather than progressive pneumonia.

evidence.reference_title: Pontiac fever. An epidemic of unknown etiology in a health department: I. Clinical and epidemiologic aspects.
evidence.snippet: Illness characterized principally by fever, headache, myalgia, and malaise affected at least 144 persons, including 95 of 100 persons employed in the health department building.

evidence.reference_title: The epidemiology of Legionella pneumophila infections.
evidence.snippet: Pontiac fever is an acute, self-limited, febrile illness with an attack rate of 95% to 100% and an incubation period of 36 hours.

nodes.description: Chills may accompany the febrile outbreak syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: An outbreak of Pontiac fever related to whirlpool use, Michigan 1982.
evidence.snippet: Fourteen of 23 female members of a church group experienced an acute self-limited illness characterized by chills, fever, chest pain, cough, and nausea, consistent with the diagnosis of Pontiac fever.

nodes.description: Fever is a cardinal component of the acute Pontiac fever syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Pontiac fever. An epidemic of unknown etiology in a health department: I. Clinical and epidemiologic aspects.
evidence.snippet: Illness characterized principally by fever, headache, myalgia, and malaise affected at least 144 persons, including 95 of 100 persons employed in the health department building.

nodes.description: Headache is part of the core influenza-like symptom complex.

evidence.reference_title: Pontiac fever. An epidemic of unknown etiology in a health department: I. Clinical and epidemiologic aspects.
evidence.snippet: Illness characterized principally by fever, headache, myalgia, and malaise affected at least 144 persons, including 95 of 100 persons employed in the health department building.

nodes.description: General malaise is part of the acute systemic syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Pontiac fever. An epidemic of unknown etiology in a health department: I. Clinical and epidemiologic aspects.
evidence.snippet: Illness characterized principally by fever, headache, myalgia, and malaise affected at least 144 persons, including 95 of 100 persons employed in the health department building.

nodes.description: Muscle pain occurs as part of the acute influenza-like illness.

evidence.reference_title: Pontiac fever. An epidemic of unknown etiology in a health department: I. Clinical and epidemiologic aspects.
evidence.snippet: Illness characterized principally by fever, headache, myalgia, and malaise affected at least 144 persons, including 95 of 100 persons employed in the health department building.

nodes.description: After common-source Legionella exposure, Pontiac fever can occur as the mild non-pneumonic clinical expression while other exposed persons develop pneumonic Legionnaires disease.

evidence.reference_title: Pneumonic and nonpneumonic forms of legionellosis. The result of a common-source exposure to Legionella pneumophila.
evidence.snippet: In one man severe, life-threatening Legionnaires' disease developed, whereas the other experienced a comparatively mild, self-limiting illness that was consistent with previous descriptions of cases of Pontiac fever.

evidence.reference_title: Hot tub legionellosis. Legionnaires' disease and Pontiac fever after a point-source exposure to Legionella pneumophila.
evidence.snippet: Legionella pneumophila is associated with outbreaks of either Pontiac fever, a self-limited influenzalike condition without pneumonia, or Legionnaires' disease, a severe pneumonic disease affecting elderly or immunocompromised individuals.

nodes.description: A normal chest radiograph supports the non-pneumonic clinical distinction in compatible cases, although diagnosis still depends on exposure and laboratory/epidemiologic context.
nodes.id: Normal chest radiograph in Pontiac fever case

nodes.description: Because typical Pontiac fever is self-limited and non-pneumonic, management is framed as symptom-directed supportive care for uncomplicated cases rather than routine disease-specific antimicrobial therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Pontiac fever: isolation of the etiologic agent (Legionella pneumophilia) and demonstration of its mode of transmission.
evidence.snippet: Pontiac fever, a unique epidemiologic form of legionellosis, is characterized by a short (one- to two-day) incubation period and a self-limited grippe-like illness without pneumonia.

evidence.reference_title: Pontiac fever. An epidemic of unknown etiology in a health department: I. Clinical and epidemiologic aspects.
evidence.snippet: Illness was self-limited, generally lasting from two to five days.
